explanation.title: Controlling Element Layout with Display and Position

codeExample.title: Positioned Notification Badge
codeExample.code:
CodeGuru AI — Navigation (sticky)

Notification Badges

The red badges use position: absolute on a position: relative parent.

📬 3
🔔 12
codeExample.explanation:
- `position: sticky; top: 0` on `nav` — scrolls with the page until it hits the top, then sticks there
- `z-index: 10` on `nav` — keeps the nav above any content that scrolls underneath it
- `position: relative` on `.icon-wrapper` — establishes the positioning context for the badge inside it
- `position: absolute; top: -6px; right: -10px` on `.badge` — anchors the badge to the top-right corner of the wrapper
- `position: fixed; bottom: 24px; right: 24px` on `.fab` — locks the button to the viewport corner regardless of scroll

exercise.title: Create a Card with an Absolute Badge
exercise.instructions:
Build a product card with an absolute-positioned sale badge. Create a div with class 'card' that has position relative, a border, padding 20px, max-width 260px, and border-radius 8px. Inside it add a span with class 'sale-badge' that has position absolute, top 0, right 0, background-color #ef4444, color white, padding 4px 10px, and border-radius 0 8px 0 8px. Also add an h3 product name and a p price inside the card.

Expected: card with a red badge in the top-right corner.
exercise.starterCode:
SALE

Mechanical Keyboard

₹2499 ₹3999
exercise.solutionCode:
SALE

Mechanical Keyboard

₹2499 ₹3999
hints: 💡 Add position: relative to .card so the badge can position itself inside it.
💡 Add position: absolute; top: 0; right: 0; to .sale-badge to anchor it to the top-right of the card.
💡 border-radius: 0 8px 0 8px sets corners individually: top-left, top-right, bottom-right, bottom-left.

questions.question: What must a parent element have for position: absolute to work predictably on a child?
options: display: block
position: relative (or absolute, fixed, or sticky)
overflow: hidden
z-index: 1
questions.explanation: An absolutely positioned element is placed relative to its nearest ancestor that has a position other than static. If no such ancestor exists, it positions itself relative to the initial viewport. Adding position: relative to the parent element (without changing its visual position) creates the positioning context the child needs.

questions.question: What is the difference between position: fixed and position: sticky?
options: They are identical — both fix the element to the viewport
Fixed is always at the same viewport position; sticky scrolls normally then sticks at a threshold
Sticky is always at the same viewport position; fixed scrolls normally then sticks
Fixed only works horizontally; sticky only works vertically
questions.explanation: position: fixed removes the element from document flow and fixes it at a specific viewport position regardless of scrolling — it never moves. position: sticky keeps the element in the document flow and scrolls with the page normally until it reaches the threshold set by top/bottom/left/right, at which point it sticks until its parent scrolls out of view.

explanation.title: Arranging Elements in One Direction with Flexbox
explanation.content:
# CSS Flexbox Before Flexbox, centering a div vertically in CSS was famously difficult. Flexbox (Flexible Box Layout) changed everything. It lets you arrange items in a row or column, distribute space between them, and align them — all in a few lines of CSS. ## Activating Flexbox Add `display: flex` to a **container** element. All its direct children become **flex items**: ```html
Item 1
Item 2
Item 3
``` ```css .container { display: flex; } ``` ## The Two Axes Flexbox works along two axes: - **Main axis** — the direction items flow (default: horizontal, left to right) - **Cross axis** — perpendicular to the main axis (default: vertical) `flex-direction` sets the main axis: | Value | Main axis direction | |---|---| | `row` | Left to right (default) | | `row-reverse` | Right to left | | `column` | Top to bottom | | `column-reverse` | Bottom to top | ## Alignment Properties ```css .container { display: flex; flex-direction: row; /* Main axis alignment */ justify-content: flex-start; /* default */ /* flex-end | center | space-between | space-around | space-evenly */ /* Cross axis alignment */ align-items: stretch; /* default */ /* flex-start | flex-end | center | baseline */ /* Wrap when items overflow */ flex-wrap: nowrap; /* default */ /* wrap | wrap-reverse */ gap: 16px; /* space between flex items */ } ``` ## Flex Item Properties ```css .item { flex: 1; /* shorthand: grow=1, shrink=1, basis=0 */ flex-grow: 1; /* how much extra space to absorb */ flex-shrink: 0; /* do not shrink when space is tight */ align-self: center; /* override container's align-items for this item only */ } ``` `flex: 1` on all items distributes available space equally. `flex: 2` on one item gives it twice as much space as the others. In the code example below, you will see `justify-content` and `align-items` demonstrated with a navigation bar and a centred hero section.

codeExample.title: Flexbox Navigation and Hero
codeExample.code:
CodeGuru AI
Home Courses Pricing

Learn to Code in India

AI-powered lessons in Python, JavaScript, Java, and HTML/CSS.

Learn

Expert-made lessons with live examples.

Practice

Hands-on coding in every lesson.

Progress

Track your XP and streak daily.
codeExample.explanation:
- `justify-content: space-between` on `nav` — pushes the logo to the left edge and links to the right edge
- `align-items: center` on `nav` — vertically centres both the logo and the link group in the nav bar
- `flex-direction: column` on `.hero` — stacks children vertically instead of horizontally
- `justify-content: center; align-items: center` on `.hero` — centres children both vertically and horizontally
- `gap: 16px` — adds uniform spacing between flex items without needing margins
- `flex: 1` on `.card` — each card grows equally to fill the available width

exercise.title: Build a Flexbox Pricing Row
exercise.instructions:
Create a row of three pricing cards using Flexbox. The container div must have display flex, gap 24px, and padding 40px. Each card (class 'plan') must use flex: 1, have a border, border-radius 10px, padding 24px, and text-align center. The middle card should have background-color #22c55e and color white to highlight it. Each card needs an h3 plan name and a p description.

Expected: three equal-width cards side by side, middle one green.

hints: 💡 Add display: flex; gap: 24px; padding: 40px; to .plans to activate Flexbox layout.
💡 Add flex: 1; border: 1px solid #e2e8f0; border-radius: 10px; padding: 24px; text-align: center; to .plan.
💡 The .plan.featured rule targets elements that have both classes — no extra HTML changes needed.

questions.question: Which property do you add to a container to make it a flex container?
options: flex: 1
display: flex
flexbox: enabled
flex-direction: row
questions.explanation: display: flex applied to a container element activates Flexbox. All of its direct children automatically become flex items. flex: 1 is a property for flex items, not the container. flex-direction: row is a container property but only controls the direction of an already-activated flex container.

questions.question: What does justify-content: space-between do in a flex container?
options: Centres all items vertically
Adds equal space around all items
Places the first item at the start, last at the end, with equal gaps between
Makes all items the same width
questions.explanation: justify-content: space-between distributes items along the main axis so the first item is flush with the start, the last item is flush with the end, and remaining space is distributed equally between the items. Compare with space-around (equal space on both sides of each item) and space-evenly (equal space between all items and edges).

questions.question: What does flex: 1 do when applied to multiple flex items?
options: Sets each item's width to 1 pixel
Makes each item take up one row
Allows each item to grow equally and share available space
Fixes each item at the first position
questions.explanation: flex: 1 is shorthand for flex-grow: 1, flex-shrink: 1, flex-basis: 0. When multiple items all have flex: 1, they share the available space equally. If one item has flex: 2 and two others have flex: 1, the first item takes half the space and the others share the remaining half.

codeExample.explanation:
- `display: flex` on `.page-layout` with no `flex-direction` — items flow in a row (sidebar left, main right)
- `flex-shrink: 0` on `.sidebar` — prevents the sidebar from shrinking when the window narrows
- `flex: 1` on `.main` — the main content area absorbs all remaining horizontal space
- `flex-wrap: wrap` on `.grid` — allows cards to drop to a new row when they cannot fit
- `flex: 0 0 calc(50% - 8px)` on `.course-card` — sets grow=0, shrink=0, basis=half width minus half the 16px gap

exercise.title: Build a Two-Column Profile Layout
exercise.instructions:
Create a two-column layout using Flexbox. The outer container (class 'layout') needs display flex, gap 24px, padding 40px, and background #f8fafc. The left column (class 'sidebar') needs width 200px, flex-shrink 0, white background, border-radius 10px, and padding 20px. The right column (class 'content') needs flex 1, white background, border-radius 10px, and padding 20px.

Expected: sidebar fixed at 200px, content fills remaining space.
exercise.starterCode:

exercise.solutionCode:

hints: 💡 Add display: flex; gap: 24px; padding: 40px; background: #f8fafc; to .layout to activate the Flexbox row.
💡 Add width: 200px; flex-shrink: 0; to .sidebar so it stays at 200px and never shrinks.
💡 Add flex: 1; to .content so it absorbs all the remaining space not taken by the sidebar.

questions.question: What does flex-shrink: 0 prevent a flex item from doing?
options: It prevents the item from growing when there is extra space
It prevents the item from shrinking when the container is too small
It prevents the item from wrapping to a new line
It prevents the item from accepting padding
questions.explanation: By default, flex items have flex-shrink: 1, which allows them to shrink below their natural or specified size when the container runs out of space. Setting flex-shrink: 0 on an item like a sidebar prevents it from shrinking — it will always maintain its declared width even when the screen narrows.

questions.question: What does flex-wrap: wrap do in a flex container?
options: It wraps text inside each flex item
It allows flex items to flow onto multiple lines when they cannot fit on one line
It reverses the order of items
It makes all items the same height
questions.explanation: By default, flex containers force all items onto a single line and shrink them to fit. Adding flex-wrap: wrap allows items to break onto additional lines when there is not enough space. This is essential for responsive card grids — at wide screens all cards fit in one row; on narrow screens they wrap into two or more rows.
